result:
It begins, as so many things do, with a walk. Not a purposeful, heart-rate-monitoring, calorie-burning walk, but a slow, meandering, almost pointless one. The kind where your feet decide the direction and your mind is just a passive, somewhat distracted passenger. I stepped out because the walls of the room had started to hum with a low-frequency pressure, the pressure of unwritten words, unanswered emails, and the unyielding glare of a blank page. The air inside was stale with thought. Outside, it was that interstitial hour—not quite afternoon, not quite evening. The sun was a softened coin behind a veil of high cloud, casting the world in a uniform, gentle pewter light. It was the light of waiting.

My feet took me past the regimented flower beds of the neighbourhood, past the identical porches, and toward a fringe of land I knew was there but rarely visited: a thin strip of forgotten woods bordering a sluggish creek. This wasn’t wilderness; it was a leftover. A seam of untidiness between two subdivisions, probably owned by the city, maintained by neglect. I stepped off the asphalt and onto a spongy mat of decaying leaves.

The silence here was different. It wasn’t an absence of sound, but a change in its texture. The distant highway drone became a faint, oceanic sigh. The wind, which on the street was an annoyance, here had a job to do. It whispered through the bare branches of the river birch, making them click together like polite, skeletal applause. It ruffled the evergreen leaves of the invasive ivy smothering a fallen oak. I stood still, and the world began to animate itself around my stillness.

A rustle in the leaf litter wasn’t just a rustle; it was the frantic, armoured scrambling of an Eastern fence lizard, its body the perfect colour of dried bark. It froze, one jewel-black eye fixed on me, its sides pulsing. We were both intruders in our own way. Above, a crow cawed—a sound so harsh and pragmatic it seemed to stitch the softer sounds together. Then, a faint, almost melodic trickle: the creek, mostly hidden by a thicket of brambles.

I pushed toward the sound, my city-soft hands snagging on thorns. There’s a particular smell to such a place—wet earth, rotting wood, the faint tannic scent of stagnant water, and beneath it all, the cold, mineral smell of approaching winter. I found the creek, narrow enough to jump across. The water was the colour of weak tea, stained by the leaves it stewed. It moved with a profound laziness around a shopping cart, its rusted skeleton half-submerged, a plastic bag caught on its handle like a ghostly flag. A monument to carelessness. Yet, nature was busy reclaiming it. Algae coated its lower bars in a fuzzy green sweater. A water strider skated unconcerned in the eddy behind it.

I found a relatively dry log and sat. This is when the real shift happened. The walk had quieted my body; now the sitting began to quiet the clamour in my head. The to-do list, the clever argument I should have made in yesterday’s meeting, the low-grade anxiety about things I cannot control—they didn’t vanish, but they lost their urgency. They became like the distant highway noise: present, but transformed, part of the backdrop.

My attention, that frantic searchlight usually sweeping for problems or entertainment, settled. It landed on the fractal patterns of the moss on the log, a miniature, vibrant forest of its own. It traced the path of a single fallen leaf, caramel-coloured and veined like a hand, as it rode a tiny current, twirling in a slow, doomed waltz before catching on a twig. I watched a spider, patient as stone, in the center of a web so perfect it seemed etched on the air. The web trembled with secrets I couldn’t perceive—the footfall of a gnat, the shift in the humidity.

This, I thought, is the real news. Not the headlines screaming from my phone, but this silent, relentless process of living and dying, of growth and decay, happening at a speed so glacial and so swift it defies human measurement. The shopping cart and the spider’s web were not in conflict; they were simply both true. One spoke of human time—a brief burst of use, then abandonment. The other spoke of a different time, a cyclical, patient time that wraps around our own like the ivy around the oak, eventually digesting it.

I thought of the word “sublime.” Not as in “that cake was sublime,” but in the old, Romantic sense—the feeling of awe and terror in the face of nature’s overwhelming power and beauty. This wasn’t the sublime of a thunderstorm or a mountain peak. This was the *quiet sublime*. The awe of intricate, unnoticed systems. The terror in the spider’s perfect, predatory patience. The beauty in the green fuzz on the rusted metal. It was a reminder that my narrative, the story of *me* that runs constantly in my head, is just a tiny, flickering subplot in a story so vast it has no narrator.

A chill seeped up from the log into my bones. The pewter light was deepening into blue. The evening star, Venus, pricked through the grey canopy of cloud directly above the creek. It was time to go back. Back to walls, to light switches, to the hum of the refrigerator and the glow of screens.

I rose, stiff, and made my way out of the thicket. As I reached the asphalt, the spell didn’t so much break as change form. The sensory overload of the human world rushed back: the smell of someone’s dinner, the precise geometry of streetlights clicking on, the concrete solid under my shoes.

But I carried the quiet with me, a still pool in my centre. The walk had been an act of listening. Not for anything in particular, but listening as a state of being. I had listened to the creek, to the wind, to the lizard’s heartbeat. And in that listening, my own internal noise had lost its power to tyrannize.

I didn’t return with any grand epiphany, no solved problems or lyrical truths. I returned with damp shoes and a smudge of dirt on my knee. I returned with the memory of a leaf’s slow twirl and the geometry of a spider’s web. I returned knowing, in a way that isn’t intellectual but cellular, that the world is layered. That beneath our layer of urgent schedules and digital ghosts, there is an older, slower, more patient layer. It is always there, in the leftover places, by the sluggish creeks, doing its work. You don’t have to find peace there, or answers. You only have to sit on a log, be cold, and listen. It continues with or without you, and there is a profound relief in that. The relief of being, for a moment, not the author of the story, but a character in it—and a very small, temporary one at that. The walk was over. The silence, however, had come home with me.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
